quote: what we do here at ION is I would just ask you a couple questions so we can build a design that has actual details
stewart_read: Pedro has just stated a clear channel preference — written information only. Meg's response does not acknowledge the preference at all. She pivots directly to her own process. The strategic read would be: Meg is deliberately not engaging the channel objection because she plans to reframe it later. The reactive read is: Meg didn't register Pedro's request as something requiring acknowledgment. The reactive read is supported by the fact that she never returns to the channel preference with any alternative — she simply restates Ion's process twice more before terminating.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg deliberately bypassed the channel preference to test whether Pedro would engage on process — a calculated move to see if the objection was real.
coaching_implication: Show Meg the gap between 'I totally get you' (01:42) and what she actually did (ignored the request). The coaching move is not 'be more empathetic' — it is: when a customer states a channel preference, name it back before redirecting. Suggested rephrase intent: 'You want to see something in writing before we talk further — that makes sense. Here's the thing: the written design is exactly what our specialist builds. The 10-minute call is how we get you the written version.' Acknowledge the preference, then bridge to the process.

quote: That's it. That's step one. And and from there, we'll see where it goes. If you're willing to do that, fine. If not, no problem.
stewart_read: Pedro is not refusing solar — he is proposing a sequential engagement model. 'Step one' and 'from there we'll see' is a conditional openness signal: he is willing to move forward if Meg can meet him at his starting point. This is the highest-value moment in the call. Meg's response ('Yeah. We can do that. There's just information I need to gather') acknowledges the conditional framing but immediately overrides it with Ion's process, collapsing the conditional opening.
alternative_hypothesis: Pedro's 'if not, no problem' is a soft exit signal and the conditional framing is a polite way of saying he is not interested.
coaching_implication: Pedro's 'step one' framing is a workable entry point. The manager should replay this moment and ask Meg: what did Pedro actually offer you here? He offered a process. The coaching gap is that Meg never matched his process with a version of Ion's process that could satisfy step one — e.g., 'What if step one was a 10-minute call where I get you the specific numbers for your home, and you decide from there?' The lead was not closed; it was not met.

quote: And the system is custom made. It's not just a a cookie cutter type thing. Right?
stewart_read: Meg introduces a product feature ('custom made, not cookie cutter') unprompted. Pedro has not asked about system design — he has asked for written cost information. This is scope creep: Meg is explaining solar rather than creating curiosity or bridging to the appointment. The schema is explicit that setters should resist the urge to explain and focus on the customer's bill. This feature explanation does not address Pedro's stated concern and introduces content the closer should own.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg is using 'custom made' to justify why she needs to gather information — a process explanation, not a product pitch.
coaching_implication: Even if the intent was process justification, the execution lands as a product feature. The manager should ask Meg: did explaining 'custom made' move Pedro closer to agreeing to a call? It didn't — he immediately restated his written-information request. The coaching move is: when a customer asks for cost information, the redirect is curiosity ('the number that matters most is the one specific to your home — that's what the specialist builds'), not a feature explanation.

quote: Then give me a range of price for each thing if you can't give me a specific one.
stewart_read: Pedro explicitly requests a price range — a direct trigger for the schema's price_estimate_before_design rebuttal. The canonical response redirects to the design process by acknowledging the fairness of the ask and explaining why a range would be meaningless without home-specific data. Meg never applies this rebuttal. Instead, she admits she doesn't have price details (01:22) with no redirect — a knowledge_gap_handling failure that leaves Pedro with no path forward.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg's admission of not having price details is honest and builds trust — she is not fabricating numbers.
coaching_implication: Honesty about not having numbers is correct. The failure is the absence of a redirect. The manager should walk Meg through the price_estimate_before_design rebuttal and show her how the redirect works: 'Totally fair to ask. The thing is, a range without your home's data would be meaningless — it could be off by thousands either way. The specialist builds the actual number in 10 minutes. That's the number worth having.' The admission without the redirect is a dead end.

quote: I mean, I I myself don't have, like, any price details.
stewart_read: Meg's admission that she has no price details is accurate but unrecovered. Per the schema's knowledge_gap_handling directive, the three options are: dodge and redirect, admit and recover with transparency, or fabricate. Meg admits — which is correct — but does not recover. There is no bridge to the specialist, no curiosity hook, no alternative path offered. The admission lands as a dead end rather than a trust-building moment.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg is setting up a redirect that she never delivers — the admission was intended as a setup for 'that's why the specialist call exists.'
coaching_implication: The manager should show Meg the difference between 'I don't have price details' (dead end) and 'I don't have price details — that's exactly why the specialist call exists, because they build the actual number for your home in real time.' The admission is the right move; the missing piece is the recovery bridge. This is a one-sentence fix.

quote: I totally get you. Yes. I mean, here at Allynos, we don't really just send quotes out of the mail or anything like that
stewart_read: This is Meg's second attempt at the same angle: Ion's policy does not allow mailed quotes. She opens with 'I totally get you' — a false empathy signal — and immediately contradicts it with a policy restatement. This is the same angle she used at 00:29, now in its second iteration. No new frame, no curiosity hook, no alternative path. Note: 'Allynos' is almost certainly a Deepgram ASR artifact of 'Ion' — verify against audio before coaching on the name; the behavioral failure stands regardless of the transcription.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg is using the policy restatement as a final boundary-setting move before offering an alternative — but the alternative never comes.
coaching_implication: The manager should count the angles with Meg: angle one (00:29) — Ion's process requires questions. Angle two (01:42) — Ion doesn't mail quotes. Both are the same angle: Ion's policy. The schema calls for 3+ angles before giving up. The manager should ask: what would angle three have looked like? Suggested intent: meet Pedro's written-information preference by reframing the specialist call as the mechanism that produces the written output he wants.

quote: I think I explained to you what I would like. If if that's not clear, I'll just move on and, no problem.
stewart_read: Pedro is giving Meg a final opening — 'if that's not clear' implies he would stay if Meg demonstrated she understood his request. This is not a hard exit; it is a conditional one. Meg's response is to accept the exit ('Okay. Well, yeah. I mean, we're we're not able to do that here, so I'll just take you off my list.'). No callback offer, no specialist bridge, no acknowledgment that she heard his actual request. The lead exits with zero recovery infrastructure.
alternative_hypothesis: Pedro's 'no problem' signals genuine disengagement — he has already decided and the conditional framing is a social grace, not a real opening.
coaching_implication: The manager should ask Meg: what did you hear Pedro say at 02:09? If she heard 'I'm done,' replay the line and ask again. The coaching move is: when a customer says 'if that's not clear, I'll move on,' the correct response is to demonstrate clarity — 'I hear you: you want written information before any conversation. What if I had our specialist send you a one-page summary of what solar looks like for your address, and you decide from there whether a call makes sense?' That is a third angle. Meg never got there.

quote: Okay. Well, yeah. I mean, we're we're not able to do that here, so I'll just take you off my list.
stewart_read: Meg's exit line is a clean surrender with no recovery infrastructure. 'Take you off my list' is a permanent close — no callback, no specialist referral, no bridge. Pedro was not a hard DQ: he confirmed he filled out the form, expressed interest in solar information, and gave Meg three conditional openings. The call ends because Meg ran out of angles, not because Pedro was unqualifiable.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg correctly reads Pedro as a low-probability lead and conserves time by exiting cleanly rather than burning more minutes on a resistant customer.
coaching_implication: The manager should distinguish between a hard DQ exit and a lead-abandoned exit. Pedro meets no hard DQ criteria in the schema. The coaching move is not 'don't give up' — it is: before you exit, offer one bridge. 'I'll take you off my call list — but would it be okay if I had someone send you a one-page overview by email? No call required.' That preserves the lead without violating Pedro's channel preference. Meg never attempted it.
